title: Regression: Predicting a Number
concept: Regression models predict a continuous numeric value, like a house price, rather than sorting data into categories.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary goal of high feature density in machine learning?

options.text: To pack the maximum amount of predictive signal into the smallest number of features
To collect as many features as possible, regardless of their relevance
To display data in complex multi-dimensional charts

exercises.explain: Exactly! Simpler models with high feature density are faster, cheaper, and more robust in production.

cards.title: Pricing a home
cards.body: If you want to buy a house, you want to know its dollar value. You aren't just sorting houses into "cheap" vs. "expensive" categories — you want to predict a specific, exact price based on its size.

cards.title: What is regression?
cards.body: Regression is a type of machine learning where the model predicts a continuous, numeric value (like $345,000 or 72°F) rather than a category label (like "spam" or "not spam").

cards.title: A dimmer switch vs. on/off
cards.body: Think of classification as an on/off light switch — it's either one option or the other. Regression is like a dimmer switch: it can slide smoothly to any value along a continuous scale.

cards.title: Predicting taxi fare
cards.body: A taxi company uses regression to predict the exact fare of a ride. The model takes inputs like trip distance and time of day, outputting a precise decimal number like $18.50.

cards.title: Not just for finance
cards.body: You might think regression is only for predicting money or stock prices. In reality, it's used for any continuous number: predicting a student's test score, tomorrow's rainfall in inches, or a battery's remaining life in minutes.

cards.title: Classification vs. Regression
cards.body: How they differ based on the target output:

left.text:
Predicts discrete labels
e.g. Cat vs. Dog
or Spam vs. Safe

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a regression task?

options.text: Predicting the height of a tree in feet
Classifying an email as spam or inbox
Detecting if a credit card transaction is fraudulent (yes/no)
Grouping customers into 4 distinct target segments

exercises.explain: Predicting height in feet results in a continuous numerical value, which is a regression task.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model that predicts whether a user will click an ad (yes or no) is a regression model.
exercises.explain: False. Predicting a yes/no label is a classification task. Regression predicts continuous numeric values.

exercises.sentence: Classification outputs discrete categories, whereas regression outputs ___ numbers.
exercises.explain: Regression outputs continuous numeric values that can fall anywhere on a scale (like decimals or prices).

exercises.prompt: Which one of these outputs represents a regression model's prediction?
exercises.explain: A specific number like 24.5 minutes is continuous data, making it a regression output.

exercises.prompt: You are building a model for a ride-sharing app. It needs to calculate the estimated arrival time (ETA) in minutes for drivers. Should you frame this as a classification or regression problem, and why?

options.text: Regression, because time is a continuous number and you want to predict precise minutes and seconds.
Classification, because the model should choose from preset categories like 'Fast', 'Medium', or 'Slow'.
Regression, because you only care about whether the driver arrives on time or late.
Classification, because arrival time has no numeric value.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Time can be measured down to the second, representing a continuous numeric value. Regression is perfect for this. Next: how to draw a path through this numeric data!
